1-1.v: US GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) is issued by the FASB and used in the United States. IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards) is issued by the IASB and used in 140+ countries. The CMA exam tests BOTH — you must know where they agree and where they differ.

r: LIFO PROHIBITED — only FIFO & Weighted Average
Cost model OR Revaluation model (mark up to FV)

v: Both use ACCRUAL basis for external reporting
Both require the four core financial statements
Both use historical cost as primary measurement basis
Both follow going concern and consistency assumptions
Revenue recognition (ASC 606 / IFRS 15) now substantially converged

1-1.v: Exam Tips: LIFO = GAAP only — IFRS prohibits it. IFRS allows PP&E revaluation upward; GAAP does not. IFRS allows reversal of inventory write-downs; GAAP does not. Development costs capitalized under IFRS when feasibility established; always expensed under GAAP. Rules-based = GAAP. Principles-based = IFRS.

1-2.v: Financial statements are prepared for EXTERNAL users who cannot access internal management information. Understanding who uses the statements and what decisions they make is fundamental to financial reporting.
For financial information to be USEFUL, it must have these characteristics (FASB Conceptual Framework):

r: Information that makes a difference in decisions (materiality, predictive & confirmatory value)
Complete, neutral, and free from error
Independent observers can confirm the amounts
Available before it loses its ability to influence decisions

v: Provide information useful for INVESTMENT and CREDIT decisions
Report cash flow prospects — ability to generate future cash
Report enterprise resources, claims against them, and changes in them
Help users assess management stewardship of resources

1-2.v: Exam Tips: Primary users = investors and creditors (most important). Relevance + Faithful Representation = two FUNDAMENTAL qualitative characteristics. Comparability, Verifiability, Timeliness, Understandability = ENHANCING characteristics. Materiality = component of relevance. Information is material if omitting or misstating it could influence decisions.

r: Assets, liabilities, and equity at a POINT IN TIME
Revenues and expenses over a PERIOD

v: Net Income (IS) → increases Retained Earnings → flows to Balance Sheet equity
Dividends declared → reduces Retained Earnings on BS
Ending cash (SCF) = Cash on the Balance Sheet
Changes in BS accounts drive the adjustments in the SCF (indirect method)
Statement of Changes in Equity bridges beginning and ending equity on the BS

1-3.v: Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity | Assets = Liabilities + (Paid-in Capital + Retained Earnings + OCI)

r: Probable future economic benefits controlled by the entity
Probable future sacrifices of economic benefits
Residual interest in assets after deducting liabilities
Inflows from delivering goods/services — core operations
Outflows or consumption of assets in delivering goods/services
Increases in equity from peripheral activities
Decreases in equity from peripheral activities

1-3.v: Exam Tips: Balance Sheet = POINT IN TIME (date). IS, SCF, SCE = PERIOD (from/to dates). Net Income flows from IS to Retained Earnings on BS. Ending Cash on SCF = Cash on BS. Assets = Liabilities + Equity ALWAYS. Revenues/Gains increase equity; Expenses/Losses decrease equity.

1-4.v: An item is RECOGNIZED (recorded) in the financial statements when it meets the definition of an element AND can be measured with sufficient reliability. Recognition means including the item on the face of the statements — not just in the notes.

r: Cost to replace the asset today
Estimated selling price minus completion/selling costs
Price in an orderly transaction between market participants
Entity will continue operations indefinitely — not liquidating
Record when earned/incurred, not when cash flows
Report for regular time periods (month, quarter, year)
Express transactions in money (stable purchasing power assumed)

v: Cost-Benefit Constraint: Benefits of providing information must exceed the cost of providing it
Materiality: Only information significant enough to affect decisions needs full disclosure
Conservatism (Prudence): When uncertainty exists, choose the option least likely to overstate assets/income

1-4.v: Exam Tips: Historical cost = most common measurement basis. Fair value = for investments and impairment. NRV = inventory write-down to LCNRV. Going concern = assumed unless evidence suggests otherwise. Accrual basis = required for external reporting. Conservatism = don't overstate assets/income when uncertain.

1-5.v: RECOGNITION = recording on the face of the financial statements. DISCLOSURE = presenting in the notes. Not all items that are disclosed are recognized — e.g., contingent liabilities may only be disclosed if not probable/measurable.

v: Current Assets: Reported at the LOWER of cost or NRV (inventory) or carrying value
Investments: Reported at fair value (trading/AFS) or amortized cost (HTM)
PP&E: Historical cost minus accumulated depreciation (GAAP); or fair value under IFRS revaluation
Intangibles: Historical cost minus accumulated amortization (limited-life); indefinite-life tested for impairment
Current liabilities: Face value (AP, accrued expenses)
Long-term debt: Amortized cost (present value of future payments)
Bonds payable: Face value adjusted for unamortized premium/discount
Contingent liabilities: Recorded if probable AND reasonably estimable
Common stock: Par value × shares issued
APIC (Additional Paid-in Capital): Amount received ABOVE par value
Retained Earnings: Cumulative net income minus dividends
Accumulated OCI: Unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities, foreign currency translation, pension adjustments
Treasury Stock: Deducted from equity at COST (reduces equity)

1-5.v: Exam Tips: Contingent liability: record only when PROBABLE and ESTIMABLE. Reasonably possible = notes only. Remote = no action. Treasury stock = deducted from equity (not an asset). Bonds at amortized cost. Investments — trading & AFS at FV; HTM at amortized cost. LCNRV for inventory.

1-6.v: The Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position) presents assets, liabilities, and equity as of a SPECIFIC DATE. It is classified (current vs non-current) for most companies.

r: Currency and highly liquid investments with maturity ≤ 3 months
AR minus allowance for credit losses
Goods held for sale — FIFO, LIFO, or WA cost
Payments made for future benefits (insurance, rent)

1-6.v: Current assets are listed in ORDER OF LIQUIDITY (most liquid first).

v: PP&E (net): Historical cost minus accumulated depreciation
Intangible Assets: Patents, trademarks, goodwill — net of amortization
Long-term Investments: Equity method investments, HTM securities
Other Assets: Deferred tax assets, other long-term items
Accounts Payable: Amounts owed to suppliers
Accrued Liabilities: Expenses incurred but not yet paid (wages, interest)
Current portion of long-term debt: Due within one year
Deferred Revenue: Cash received but revenue not yet earned
Short-term borrowings: Lines of credit, notes payable < 1 year
Non-Current Liabilities: Long-term debt, deferred tax liabilities, pension obligations, long-term lease liabilities
Shareholders' Equity: Common stock (par), APIC, Retained Earnings, AOCI, minus Treasury Stock

1-6.v: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities
Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities

Exam Tips: Current = due/convertible within 1 year (or operating cycle if longer). Assets listed: most liquid first. Working Capital = CA − CL. Deferred revenue = LIABILITY (cash received, service not yet performed). Prepaid expense = ASSET (paid but not yet used). Current portion of LTD = current liability.

1-7.v: The Income Statement reports REVENUES and EXPENSES over a period, showing whether the company was profitable. It can be presented in SINGLE-STEP or MULTI-STEP format.

v: Net Sales Revenue
− Cost of Goods Sold
= Gross Profit
− Operating Expenses (SGA, R&D, Depreciation)
= Operating Income (EBIT)
± Non-operating items (Interest expense, gains/losses)
= Income Before Tax
− Income Tax Expense
= Net Income

1-7.v: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)

r: FV changes on available-for-sale investments — not yet sold
Translating foreign subsidiary FS into reporting currency
Actuarial gains/losses and prior service costs
Effective portion of cash flow hedges
Derivative hedging gains/losses until hedged transaction occurs

v: Presented SEPARATELY at the bottom of the income statement — AFTER income from continuing operations
Shown NET OF TAX
Includes: results of discontinued component + gain/loss on disposal
Component must meet specific criteria: strategic shift that has major effect on operations

1-7.v: Exam Tips: OCI items BYPASS the income statement but affect equity (AOCI). Comprehensive Income = Net Income + OCI. AFS security FV changes = OCI (not income). Trading security FV changes = NET INCOME. Discontinued operations = separate line item, net of tax. Multi-step IS: Gross Profit → Operating Income → Net Income.

r: Net Income section followed by OCI section in one document
Income Statement → then a separate Statement of Comprehensive Income starting with Net Income

1-8.v: Under both US GAAP and IFRS, companies can choose either approach. The TWO-STATEMENT approach is most common in practice.
Format of the Statement of Comprehensive Income

v: Net Income (from the Income Statement)
Other Comprehensive Income (OCI):
+ Unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities (net of tax)
+ Foreign currency translation adjustments
+ Pension adjustments (net of tax)
+ Cash flow hedge adjustments
= Total Other Comprehensive Income
= COMPREHENSIVE INCOME

1-8.v: When OCI items are eventually REALIZED (e.g., AFS security is sold), they must be RECLASSIFIED from AOCI to Net Income. A RECLASSIFICATION ADJUSTMENT prevents double-counting — the amount was in OCI before; now it moves to Net Income.

v: Example: AFS bond held for 2 years with $5,000 unrealized gain in AOCI. When sold: gain of $5,000 recognized in Net Income AND subtract $5,000 from OCI (reclassification adjustment)
Net effect: $5,000 in comprehensive income in the year of sale (via net income) — not counted again in OCI

1-8.v: Exam Tips: Both one-statement and two-statement approaches are acceptable. OCI items shown NET OF TAX. Reclassification adjustment prevents double-counting when OCI items move to Net Income. Items that NEVER get reclassified: foreign currency translation of foreign subsidiary (only reclassified on complete liquidation).

1-9.v: Statement of Changes in Stockholders' Equity
This statement shows ALL CHANGES in each component of equity during the period. It connects the beginning and ending Balance Sheet equity figures.
Notes are an INTEGRAL PART of the financial statements — not optional extras. They provide essential context and detail that cannot be presented on the face of the statements.

v: Summary of Significant Accounting Policies — ALWAYS FIRST: describes basis of accounting, inventory method, depreciation method, consolidation policy
Specific disclosures: contingencies, debt covenants, segment information, related party transactions
Detail of estimates: allowance for credit losses, useful lives of assets, pension assumptions
Fair value hierarchy disclosures (Level 1, 2, 3)
Subsequent events: significant events after BS date but before FS issuance

r: Condition EXISTED at BS date — ADJUST the FS
New condition AROSE after BS date — DISCLOSE in notes only

1-9.v: Exam Tips: Summary of significant accounting policies = ALWAYS the first note. Subsequent events: if condition existed at BS date → adjust FS. If new condition arose after BS date → disclose only. Notes are required, not optional. Fair value hierarchy: Level 1 = quoted prices (most reliable); Level 2 = observable inputs; Level 3 = unobservable (least reliable).

1-10.v: Purpose of the Statement of Cash Flows

The SCF explains the CHANGE IN CASH during a period. A company can be profitable but still run out of cash. The SCF shows WHERE cash came from and WHERE it went — essential for assessing liquidity and financial flexibility.

r: Cash from customers, cash to suppliers, wages paid, taxes paid
Purchase/sale of PP&E, purchase/sale of investments, making/collecting loans
Borrowing/repaying debt, issuing/buying back stock, paying dividends

1-10.v: Direct vs Indirect Method for Operating Activities

r: Start with Net Income, adjust for non-cash items and WC changes
Show actual cash receipts and payments
Preferred by FASB — but indirect more common; if direct used, reconciliation still required

v: Interest RECEIVED: Operating (GAAP); Operating or Investing (IFRS)
Interest PAID: Operating (GAAP); Operating or Financing (IFRS)
Dividends RECEIVED: Operating (GAAP); Operating or Investing (IFRS)
Dividends PAID: Financing (GAAP); Operating or Financing (IFRS)
Taxes paid: Operating (generally, unless specifically identifiable with investing/financing)

1-10.v: Beginning Cash + Cash from Operations + Cash from Investing + Cash from Financing = Ending Cash

Exam Tips: Interest paid = Operating (GAAP). Dividends paid = Financing (GAAP). IFRS allows more flexibility for classification. Indirect method: start with Net Income, add back depreciation, adjust for WC changes. Non-cash investing/financing disclosed separately (e.g., equipment acquired through financing).

1-11.v: The Indirect Method — Step by Step
The indirect method converts Net Income (accrual basis) to Cash from Operations. It is the most commonly used method in practice.

r: Non-cash expense — reduced income but no cash paid
Add back Loss on asset sale
Non-cash item in operations (cash from sale = investing)
Non-cash in operations (cash from sale = investing)
Sold on credit but cash not collected — earned > collected
Collected more cash than sold — collected > earned
Bought more than sold — cash used for inventory
Sold more than bought — cash inflow
Didn't pay all purchases — delayed cash outflow
Paid more than purchased — extra cash used

1-11.v: Net Income | + Non-cash charges (depreciation, amortization, impairment, loss on sale) | − Non-cash credits (gain on sale) | − Increase in current assets (AR, inventory, prepaid) | + Decrease in current assets | + Increase in current liabilities (AP, accrued) | − Decrease in current liabilities

v: Current Asset increases = USE OF CASH = SUBTRACT from Net Income
Current Asset decreases = SOURCE OF CASH = ADD to Net Income
Current Liability increases = SOURCE OF CASH = ADD to Net Income
Current Liability decreases = USE OF CASH = SUBTRACT from Net Income

1-11.v: Exam Tips: Depreciation is the MOST COMMON add-back. Gain on sale = subtract (cash from sale goes to Investing). Loss on sale = add back. AR increase = subtract (earned but not collected). AP increase = add (bought but didn't pay yet). Always adjust working capital accounts EXCLUDING cash itself.

1-12.v: Investing activities relate to LONG-TERM ASSETS and INVESTMENTS in other companies.

r: INFLOW (full proceeds, not just book value)
Making a loan to another party
Receiving dividends is NOT financing — it's Operating (GAAP)

1-12.v: When PP&E is sold, the FULL CASH PROCEEDS appear in Investing Activities — not just the book value or gain. In the Indirect Method, the gain/loss is removed from operations (as it's not an operating item), and the full proceeds go to investing.

v: Non-cash investing & financing activities disclosed SEPARATELY (supplementary schedule or footnote)
Examples: Equipment purchased by issuing note payable; land received in exchange for stock; conversion of debt to equity
Cash paid for interest and taxes must be disclosed (if indirect method used)

1-12.v: Exam Tips: Full cash PROCEEDS from PP&E sale = Investing inflow (not gain — full amount). Repayment of debt PRINCIPAL = Financing outflow (interest payment = Operating). Dividends PAID = Financing. Non-cash transactions disclosed in supplementary schedule — do NOT appear in the three main sections.

1-13.v: Integrated Reporting (IR) is a reporting framework that goes BEYOND traditional financial reporting to show how an organization creates VALUE over time across multiple capitals — not just financial capital.

v: Growing recognition that traditional FS don't capture all value drivers
IIRC (International Integrated Reporting Council) founded in 2010
Published the International IR Framework in 2013
Widely adopted especially in South Africa (mandated for JSE-listed companies)
Now overseen by the IFRS Foundation's Value Reporting Foundation

1-13.v: The Seven Guiding Principles of the IR Framework

v: 1. Strategic focus and future orientation
2. Connectivity of information (how capitals connect)
3. Stakeholder relationships
4. Materiality (what matters to the organization's value creation)
5. Conciseness
6. Reliability and completeness
7. Consistency and comparability
1. Organizational overview and external environment
2. Governance
3. Business model
4. Risks and opportunities
5. Strategy and resource allocation
6. Performance
7. Outlook
8. Basis of preparation and presentation

1-13.v: Exam Tips: 6 Capitals: Financial, Manufactured, Intellectual, Human, Social & Relationship, Natural. 7 Principles include: strategic focus, connectivity, materiality, conciseness. IR = VALUE CREATION over short, medium, and long term. Traditional FS only captures financial capital.

1-14.v: AR is reported at NET REALIZABLE VALUE (NRV) = gross AR minus the allowance for credit losses. The allowance estimates amounts that will NOT be collected.

r: Record AR at full invoice amount; record discount when taken
Record AR net of discount immediately; record discount NOT taken as revenue

1-14.v: Example (2/10, n/30 = 2% discount if paid within 10 days, full amount due in 30 days): Gross method records $10,000 AR; if customer takes discount, record $200 discount as Sales Discount.

v: GAAP requires the ALLOWANCE METHOD (not direct write-off) for GAAP financial reporting
Allowance for Credit Losses (or Allowance for Doubtful Accounts) = contra-asset to AR
Two approaches to estimate:
1. Percentage of Net Sales: Credit loss expense = % × Credit sales
2. Aging of AR: Different % applied to each age bucket — more accurate

r: Recovery of previously written off debt

1-14.v: When customers return goods or receive price reductions: Debit Sales Returns & Allowances (contra-revenue), Credit AR (or Cash).

r: Buyer assumes all credit risk — true sale
Recognize gain or loss; remove AR from BS
Seller retains credit risk — contingent liability
May be treated as borrowing; retain AR on BS

1-14.v: Exam Tips: AR on BS = Gross AR − Allowance = NET REALIZABLE VALUE. Write-off: debit Allowance, credit AR (no income statement effect when writing off). Recovery: reverse the write-off, then record cash. CECL model (current expected credit losses) = forward-looking estimate of lifetime losses. Factoring without recourse = sale. With recourse = possible borrowing.

1-15.v: Inventory is recorded at COST, which includes ALL costs to bring the inventory to its present location and condition: purchase price + freight-in + insurance in transit + import duties. EXCLUDE: freight-out (selling expense), storage after receipt, abnormal spoilage.
Which Goods Are Included in Inventory?

v: Goods in transit: FOB SHIPPING POINT → buyer's inventory (ownership transfers when shipped)
Goods in transit: FOB DESTINATION → seller's inventory until delivered
Consigned goods: Remain in CONSIGNOR's inventory (consignee does not own them)
Bill-and-hold arrangements: Remain in seller's inventory until delivery criteria met

r: Rising prices — lower COGS, higher income
Rising prices — higher COGS, lower taxes
Average cost of all units available
Exact cost of each unit sold
Exact cost of units on hand

1-15.v: LIFO Reserve = FIFO Inventory − LIFO Inventory | Convert: LIFO inventory + LIFO Reserve = FIFO inventory

r: At PERIOD END via physical count

1-15.v: Exam Tips: FOB Shipping Point → buyer owns in transit. FOB Destination → seller owns in transit. FIFO in rising prices = higher income, higher taxes. LIFO in rising prices = lower income, lower taxes (tax advantage). LIFO prohibited under IFRS. LIFO Reserve converts LIFO to FIFO equivalent. Weighted Average smooths prices.

1-16.v: Companies must perform a PHYSICAL COUNT of inventory to verify quantities on hand. Under the PERPETUAL system, the count adjusts book records to actual. Under PERIODIC, the count determines ending inventory (and thus COGS).
Inventory Errors — Effect Over Two Periods
Inventory errors are SELF-CORRECTING over two years (if no other errors). But each year's FS are misstated until corrected.

r: COGS understated → NI overstated; Assets overstated
COGS overstated → NI understated; Corrects itself
COGS overstated → NI understated; Assets understated
COGS understated → NI overstated; Corrects itself

1-16.v: Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value (LCNRV)
Inventory must be written DOWN if NRV (estimated selling price − completion/selling costs) falls below cost. GAAP uses LCNRV; IFRS uses the same concept.
Net Realizable Value = Estimated Selling Price − Estimated Costs to Complete − Estimated Selling Costs

v: If NRV < Cost → write down to NRV; recognize LOSS (cost of goods sold or write-down expense)
Under GAAP: write-down is PERMANENT — cannot be reversed
Under IFRS: write-down CAN BE REVERSED if NRV recovers (up to original cost)
LIFO/Retail methods use different market value concepts (replacement cost for LIFO)

1-16.v: Exam Tips: LCNRV — write DOWN if NRV < cost, never up. GAAP: no reversal. IFRS: reversal allowed up to original cost. Inventory error self-corrects over 2 years. Ending inventory overstated → COGS understated → NI overstated (overstates equity/RE). Remember: Ending inventory Year 1 = Beginning inventory Year 2.

1-17.v: How an investment is classified determines its ACCOUNTING TREATMENT. The classification depends on ownership percentage and INTENT.

r: <20%; no intent to hold for influence
<20%; intent to hold but not trading
Debt only; POSITIVE intent & ability to hold to maturity
Cost + share of investee NI − dividends

1-17.v: HTM securities are carried at AMORTIZED COST — original cost adjusted for premium/discount amortization. Only DEBT securities can be HTM; equity securities CANNOT.
Purchase price > Face value → Premium. Amortize (reduce) premium over life → reduces interest income
Purchase price < Face value → Discount. Amortize (increase) discount over life → increases interest income
CECL Model — Current Expected Credit Losses
Under ASC 326 (CECL), companies must estimate LIFETIME expected credit losses on HELD debt instruments at purchase — a forward-looking model. No longer wait for 'probable' threshold.

Exam Tips: TRADING → NI. AFS → OCI. HTM → amortized cost (no FV changes). Only DEBT can be HTM. Equity securities (ASC 321) always at FV through NI (no AFS for equity). AFS unrealized gains → OCI → AOCI. When AFS security sold → reclassify gain/loss from AOCI to NI (reclassification adjustment).

1-18.v: Under ASC 321, equity investments WITHOUT significant influence are measured at FAIR VALUE through NET INCOME. There is NO AFS option for equity securities (unlike debt securities).

v: Dividends received = Income (Dividend Income in NI)
Unrealized FV changes = NI each period
Exception: Equity securities without readily determinable FV may use Measurement Alternative (cost minus impairment + observable price changes)

1-18.v: When the investor has SIGNIFICANT INFLUENCE over the investee (but not control), the EQUITY METHOD is used.
Investment Account = Cost + % of Investee NI − % of Dividends − Amortization of excess FV

v: Record investor's SHARE of investee's net income → increases Investment account
Record investor's SHARE of investee's dividends received → DECREASES Investment account (dividends are a RETURN OF INVESTMENT, not income)
Amortize any excess purchase price over book value (FV differences)

1-18.v: Buy 30% of XYZ for $200,000. XYZ earns $50,000. XYZ pays $20,000 dividends. Your share: Income = $15,000 (30% × $50,000); Dividends = $6,000 (30% × $20,000). Investment account: $200,000 + $15,000 − $6,000 = $209,000.
When investor has CONTROL (generally >50% voting power), CONSOLIDATION is required. The parent and subsidiary are reported as ONE ECONOMIC ENTITY.

r: Switch to equity method; apply retroactively
Switch to consolidation; apply acquisition method
Switch to FV method; recognize any gain/loss

1-18.v: Exam Tips: <20% (no influence) → FV through NI. 20–50% (significant influence) → Equity Method. >50% (control) → Consolidation. Equity method: Dividends REDUCE investment (return of investment), NOT income. Investee NI → increases investment. Consolidation: eliminate intercompany transactions.

1-19.v: Under US GAAP (ASC 805) and IFRS (IFRS 3), ALL business combinations are accounted for using the ACQUISITION METHOD (formerly called purchase method).

v: Identify the ACQUIRER (entity that obtains control)
Determine the ACQUISITION DATE
Recognize and measure IDENTIFIABLE ASSETS AND LIABILITIES at FAIR VALUE
Recognize GOODWILL or a bargain purchase gain

1-19.v: Goodwill = Purchase Price Paid − Fair Value of Net Identifiable Assets Acquired | (FV of Net Assets = FV Assets − FV Liabilities)
Company A buys 100% of Company B for $800,000. B's FV of assets = $700,000. FV of liabilities = $200,000. FV of net assets = $500,000. Goodwill = $800,000 − $500,000 = $300,000.

v: Goodwill = INDEFINITE-LIFE intangible — NOT amortized
Tested for IMPAIRMENT annually (or when triggering events occur)
If impaired, write down (loss recognized in NI) — cannot be reversed
Under IFRS, goodwill can be allocated to cash-generating units (CGUs) for impairment testing

1-19.v: After a business combination, the PARENT (acquirer) prepares CONSOLIDATED FS combining parent and subsidiary as one entity. Intercompany transactions must be ELIMINATED.

v: Eliminate intercompany sales/purchases (parent sold to subsidiary or vice versa)
Eliminate intercompany receivables/payables
Eliminate subsidiary's equity accounts against parent's Investment account
Eliminate intercompany profit in inventory or PP&E

1-19.v: When the parent owns less than 100%, the remaining equity is NCI. NCI is shown as a SEPARATE COMPONENT of equity in the consolidated BS.

Exam Tips: Goodwill = Purchase Price − FV of net identifiable assets. Goodwill is NOT amortized — tested for impairment. Bargain purchase (FV net assets > price) = gain in NI. Intercompany transactions are ELIMINATED on consolidation. NCI = minority equity shown separately in consolidated equity.

1-20.v: Fixed assets (PP&E) are recorded at COST — all costs necessary to bring the asset to its INTENDED USE in a suitable condition and location.

v: Purchase price (net of discounts)
Import duties and non-refundable taxes
Delivery and installation costs
Site preparation costs (for land)
Professional fees (architects, engineers)
Estimated costs of dismantling/removing and restoring site (ARO — Asset Retirement Obligation)
Training costs for employees to use the asset
Administrative overheads
Repair and maintenance during use (routine)
Costs of opening a new facility (if not directly related to bringing asset into use)
Initial operating losses before commercial production

r: Appreciated; includes purchase + clearing + landscaping + legal fees
Cost includes construction + capitalized interest during construction
Parking lot, fencing, sidewalks — limited life

1-20.v: When a company constructs an asset for its OWN USE, interest on qualifying debt during the CONSTRUCTION PERIOD is CAPITALIZED (added to PP&E cost), not expensed. Capitalization ends when the asset is ready for its intended use.

Exam Tips: Land is NOT depreciated. Land Improvements ARE depreciated. Capitalized interest = interest during self-construction only. ARO cost = added to PP&E at inception. Training costs = EXPENSE. Repair & maintenance = EXPENSE. Betterments/improvements that extend useful life or add capacity = CAPITALIZE.

1-21.v: Depreciation systematically allocates the DEPRECIABLE COST of a long-lived asset over its USEFUL LIFE. Depreciable cost = Historical Cost − Salvage Value.

r: Remaining Life / Sum of Years × (Cost − Salvage)

1-21.v: Cost = $100,000. Salvage = $10,000. Life = 5 years. SL Rate = 20%. DDB Rate = 40%. Year 1: $100,000 × 40% = $40,000. Year 2: $60,000 × 40% = $24,000. NOTE: never depreciate below salvage value.

MACRS (Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System) is used for TAX purposes in the US. It uses prescribed recovery periods and accelerated methods. MACRS IGNORES salvage value. Creates TEMPORARY DIFFERENCES between book and tax → deferred taxes.
An asset is IMPAIRED when its carrying amount is NOT RECOVERABLE. Test when impairment indicators exist (significant decline in market value, adverse change in use, etc.).

v: GAAP 2-step test: 1) Recoverability test: if Carrying Value > Sum of Undiscounted Future Cash Flows → impaired; 2) Measure impairment: write down to FAIR VALUE (or PV of future CF)
IFRS: Compare Carrying Value to Recoverable Amount (higher of FV less costs of disposal OR Value in Use); write down to Recoverable Amount
Impairment loss recognized in Net Income
GAAP: no reversal. IFRS: reversal PERMITTED in later periods (except goodwill)

1-21.v: Exam Tips: SL = equal expense. DDB = accelerated (2× SL rate applied to book value, ignores salvage). SYD = accelerated but diminishes faster. MACRS = tax depreciation — ignores salvage, prescribed periods. Impairment: GAAP = 2-step (undiscounted then FV). IFRS = compare to recoverable amount. GAAP impairment: NO reversal. IFRS: reversal allowed (not goodwill).

1-22.v: Intangible assets lack physical substance but have long-term economic value: patents, trademarks, copyrights, customer lists, trade names, non-compete agreements, and GOODWILL.

r: Capitalized at COST (purchase price + legal fees)
Recognized at FAIR VALUE at acquisition date
GAAP: EXPENSED as incurred. IFRS: Research = expense; Development = capitalize when feasibility established
GAAP: Expense before technological feasibility; capitalize after
Capitalize costs after preliminary project stage

v: Amortize over USEFUL LIFE (typically straight-line unless another method better reflects consumption)
Patent: legal life 20 years — amortize over SHORTER of legal or economic life
Copyright: legal life = author's life + 70 years — amortize over economic useful life
Franchise: amortize over franchise agreement term
Trademarks with indefinite useful life — NOT amortized
Tested for IMPAIRMENT annually or when events indicate possible impairment
Impairment: carry value > FV → write down to FV, recognize loss in NI
Only recognized when arising from a BUSINESS COMBINATION
Not amortized — tested for impairment annually (ASC 350)
Private companies may elect to amortize goodwill over 10 years (optional simplified method)
Goodwill impairment: carrying value > FV → write down to FV (single-step test under ASU 2017-04)

1-22.v: Exam Tips: R&D (general) = always expensed under GAAP. Goodwill = not amortized (public companies), tested annually. Impairment loss = write down to FV. Patent = capitalized at cost; amortized over shorter of legal/economic life. Trademarks with indefinite life = not amortized. IFRS: development phase costs capitalized when feasibility met.

1-23.v: When Is Long-Term Debt Reclassified as Current?

Long-term debt is reclassified as a CURRENT LIABILITY when it becomes DUE WITHIN ONE YEAR of the Balance Sheet date. This is important for liquidity analysis — users need to know which obligations require near-term cash.

v: Any portion of long-term debt due within 12 months from the BS date → reclassify to current liabilities
Balloon payments, scheduled installments — each portion due within 1 year = current

1-23.v: When a company VIOLATES a debt covenant (e.g., minimum current ratio, maximum debt-to-equity), the lender may have the right to demand immediate repayment. This accelerates the debt → RECLASSIFY to current.

r: Covenant violated BEFORE BS date; lender has right to accelerate
CURRENT liability (demand could occur within 1 year)
Covenant violated before BS date; but lender waives right after BS date (before FS issuance)
Can remain LONG-TERM if waiver period > 12 months from BS date
Refinancing agreement signed BEFORE BS date for long-term replacement
Refinancing completed AFTER BS date but before FS issuance
Under GAAP: CURRENT (refinancing after BS date doesn't help). Under IFRS: may stay long-term

1-23.v: Under GAAP, if debt is refinanced on a long-term basis AFTER the BS date, it stays CURRENT. Under IFRS, if refinancing is completed before the FS are authorized for issue (even if after BS date), it can be classified as NON-CURRENT. Important exam difference.

Exam Tips: Reclassify to current if: matures within 1 year OR covenant violated without waiver. GAAP: refinancing must be completed BEFORE BS date to avoid current classification. IFRS: refinancing before FS authorized = can be non-current. Waivers: must cover 12 months from BS date to stay long-term.

r: Assures product works as promised — does not add a service beyond the sale
Expense as incurred (accrue estimated warranty liability)
Provides an additional service beyond the basic product warranty — customer can often buy separately
Treat as SEPARATE PERFORMANCE OBLIGATION — defer revenue and recognize over service period

1-24.v: Most basic product warranties are ASSURANCE-TYPE. The seller must estimate the future warranty costs and accrue a WARRANTY LIABILITY at the time of sale (matching principle).

v: At sale: Dr. Warranty Expense / Cr. Warranty Liability (estimated)
When claim paid: Dr. Warranty Liability / Cr. Cash or Parts Used

1-24.v: Sell 1,000 products at $100 each. Estimate 5% will be returned for warranty work at average cost of $30. Warranty expense = 1,000 × 5% × $30 = $1,500. Entry: Dr. Warranty Expense $1,500 / Cr. Warranty Liability $1,500.

When a warranty provides an ADDITIONAL SERVICE (e.g., extended warranty sold separately, or warranty covering more than assurance of basic functionality), it is treated as a SEPARATE PERFORMANCE OBLIGATION under ASC 606.

v: Allocate transaction price between product and extended warranty
Recognize warranty revenue OVER THE EXTENDED WARRANTY PERIOD (straight-line or pattern of service delivery)
Recognize warranty costs as incurred during the warranty period

1-24.v: Exam Tips: Assurance warranty = accrue at time of sale (Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Warranty Liability). Service warranty = defer revenue, recognize over warranty period. Key question: Can the customer buy the warranty separately? If yes → service-type. Matching principle drives assurance warranty accounting.

1-25.v: BOOK INCOME (accounting income) follows GAAP. TAXABLE INCOME follows the tax code (IRC). They DIFFER because some items are recognized in different periods for book vs tax purposes → creating DEFERRED TAXES.

r: Recognized in DIFFERENT PERIODS for book vs tax — will REVERSE over time
Creates Deferred Tax Asset or Liability
NEVER reverses — tax treatment and book treatment are permanently different
NO deferred tax; only affects effective tax rate
Deferred Tax LIABILITY (taxable income < book income early)
Deferred Tax ASSET (book expense > tax deduction early)
Taxable income < book income (will pay MORE tax later)
Taxable income > book income (will pay LESS tax later — prepaid)

1-25.v: Income Tax Expense = Current Tax Payable + Change in Deferred Tax Liability − Change in Deferred Tax Asset

If it is MORE LIKELY THAN NOT (>50%) that some or all of a DTA will NOT be realized (company won't have enough future taxable income), a VALUATION ALLOWANCE is recorded: Dr. Income Tax Expense / Cr. Valuation Allowance (contra-asset).

v: NOL Carryforward: Unused tax losses carried forward to offset future taxable income (indefinite carryforward under TCJA 2017, limited to 80% of taxable income)
Creates a Deferred Tax ASSET (future tax savings)
Subject to valuation allowance if realization uncertain

1-25.v: Exam Tips: Accelerated depreciation → DTL (paid less tax now, more later). Warranty expense → DTA (paid more tax now, less later). Permanent differences: tax-exempt interest, meals & entertainment, life insurance = NO deferred tax. Valuation allowance: >50% chance DTA won't be realized. NOL carryforward → DTA.

1-26.v: A contract contains a lease if it conveys the RIGHT TO CONTROL THE USE of an identified asset for a period of time in exchange for consideration. The lessee has both: (1) the right to obtain substantially all economic benefits, AND (2) the right to direct the use of the asset.
Two Types of Leases for Lessees

r: Meets any of 5 criteria (ownership transfer, purchase option, 75%+ life, 90%+ FV, specialized asset)
ROU Asset amortized (SL); separate interest expense — front-loaded total expense
Does not meet finance lease criteria
Single straight-line LEASE EXPENSE each period; ROU asset amortized as a 'plug'

v: Both finance and operating leases: RECOGNIZE on Balance Sheet at commencement
Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset = Present Value of lease payments + initial direct costs + prepaid rent
Lease Liability = Present Value of future lease payments (using incremental borrowing rate or rate implicit in lease)
KEY CHANGE from old rules: Operating leases NOW on balance sheet (previously off-balance-sheet)

r: Amortized (usually SL over shorter of life or lease term)
Reduced as 'plug' (total lease cost minus interest on liability)
Single lease expense (SL) — no separate interest

v: Finance/Sales-type lease: derecognize asset; recognize net investment in lease
Operating lease: keep asset on books; recognize lease income SL

1-26.v: Exam Tips: ASC 842 brought operating leases onto the balance sheet (ROU Asset + Lease Liability). Finance lease: front-loaded expense (interest + amortization). Operating lease: straight-line single expense. 5 criteria — if ANY met → finance lease. PV of lease payments = lease liability = ROU asset (approx). Short-term leases (<12 months) can be excluded from balance sheet.

r: Proceeds above par value from stock issuances
Cumulative net income minus dividends since inception
Cumulative OCI items: unrealized AFS gains, FX translation, pension
Cost of shares repurchased — DEDUCTED from equity

1-27.v: Ending RE = Beginning RE + Net Income − Dividends Declared ± Prior Period Adjustments

v: Three important dates:
Declaration Date: Liability created — Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Dividends Payable
Record Date: Determines WHO receives the dividend (no entry)
Payment Date: Cash paid — Dr. Dividends Payable / Cr. Cash

r: FV transferred from RE to Paid-in Capital
RE decreases, APIC increases; total equity unchanged
Par value only transferred from RE to Common Stock
RE decreases, Common Stock increases; total equity unchanged

1-27.v: Companies may RESTRICT (appropriate) part of retained earnings for specific purposes (bond covenants, legal requirements). Appropriated RE is disclosed in notes — it does NOT represent cash. It simply reduces the amount available for dividends.

Corrections of MATERIAL ERRORS from prior periods are made as PRIOR PERIOD ADJUSTMENTS to the BEGINNING BALANCE of Retained Earnings — NOT through the current income statement. Comparative FS are restated.

Exam Tips: Cash dividend: Declaration creates liability (Dr RE, Cr Div Payable). Stock dividend: small = FV to APIC; large = par value to Common Stock. Both types of stock dividends do NOT change total equity — just reclassify among equity accounts. Prior period error = adjust beginning RE, restate prior FS.

r: Maximum shares the company is permitted to issue (in charter)
Shares that have been sold to shareholders (= outstanding + treasury)
Issued shares minus treasury shares (currently held by shareholders)
Issued shares repurchased by the company — not outstanding

1-28.v: When stock is issued for cash above par: Dr. Cash / Cr. Common Stock (par × shares) + Cr. APIC (excess)
When stock is issued for NON-CASH consideration: record at FAIR VALUE of consideration received, or FV of stock issued if more reliable.

v: Reduce market price per share to make it more affordable to investors
Increase liquidity and trading volume
No economic substance — just divides existing equity into more pieces

1-28.v: Exam Tips: Stock SPLIT = no journal entry (memo only); par value per share decreases. Stock DIVIDEND = journal entry; par value unchanged; RE decreases. Outstanding = Issued − Treasury. EPS = Net Income / Weighted Average Outstanding Shares (adjust for splits retroactively in all periods).

1-29.v: Preferred stock has PREFERENCE over common stock in: (1) dividend distributions, and (2) liquidation proceeds. However, preferred shareholders generally have NO voting rights.

r: Unpaid dividends accumulate (dividends in ARREARS) — must be paid before common dividends
Missed dividends are LOST — do not accumulate
Can participate in excess dividends beyond the stated rate, along with common stock
Limited to the stated dividend rate only
Can be converted into common shares at holder's option
Company can repurchase at a specified call price
CLASSIFIED AS LIABILITY (substance over form — not really equity)

1-29.v: CUMULATIVE preferred: if dividends are missed, they ACCUMULATE as 'dividends in arrears.' These are NOT a liability until declared, but must be DISCLOSED in notes. Must be paid before common shareholders receive anything.

Preferred: 1,000 shares, $100 par, 6% cumulative. In arrears 2 years. Current year dividend = $50,000. Preferred gets: 2 years arrears × $6,000 + current year $6,000 = $18,000 first. Common gets: $50,000 − $18,000 = $32,000.

v: Issue: Dr. Cash / Cr. Preferred Stock (par) / Cr. APIC-Preferred (excess)
Retirement at call price above par: excess is a charge to retained earnings (or APIC if available)

1-29.v: Exam Tips: Cumulative = arrears accumulate. Non-cumulative = missed dividends lost. Dividends in arrears = NOT a liability until declared — DISCLOSE in notes. Preferred gets PRIORITY in both dividends and liquidation over common. Mandatorily redeemable preferred = LIABILITY not equity.

1-30.v: Treasury stock = shares that were previously ISSUED and OUTSTANDING but have been REPURCHASED by the company. They are ISSUED but NOT OUTSTANDING. They have no voting rights, receive no dividends, and are deducted from equity.

v: PURCHASE: Dr. Treasury Stock (at cost) / Cr. Cash — Treasury Stock is a CONTRA-EQUITY account (deducted from equity)
REISSUE ABOVE COST: Dr. Cash / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost) / Cr. APIC-Treasury (excess above cost)
REISSUE BELOW COST: Dr. Cash / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost) — difference: Dr. APIC-Treasury (if available), then Dr. Retained Earnings
RETIRE Treasury Stock: Dr. Common Stock (par) / Dr. APIC (original) / Dr. RE (if cost > proceeds) / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost)
REDUCES total shareholders' equity (contra-equity)
REDUCES outstanding shares (but not issued shares)
Reduces EPS denominator (fewer shares outstanding)
NO gain or loss recognized on treasury stock transactions (equity transaction, not income statement)

1-30.v: Exam Tips: Treasury stock = CONTRA-EQUITY (deducted from equity, NOT an asset). No gain/loss on treasury stock transactions (any excess/shortfall goes to APIC or RE). Cost method: record at cost paid. Reissue above cost → APIC-Treasury. Reissue below cost → reduce APIC-Treasury first, then RE. EPS uses OUTSTANDING shares (not issued).

1-31.v: ASC 606 replaced dozens of industry-specific revenue standards with ONE unified 5-step model. The core principle: recognise revenue when (or as) goods or services are TRANSFERRED to the customer in an amount reflecting the consideration the entity EXPECTS TO RECEIVE.

r: Identify the CONTRACT with a customer
Is there a valid, enforceable agreement with commercial substance?
What distinct goods or services has the company promised?
How much total consideration does the company expect to receive?
ALLOCATE the transaction price to POs
How much revenue belongs to each distinct obligation?
RECOGNISE revenue when (or as) each PO is satisfied
Has control transferred to the customer?

v: Must be approved by both parties
Each party's rights are identifiable
Payment terms are identifiable
Contract has commercial substance
Collection is probable

1-31.v: A performance obligation (PO) is a DISTINCT good or service. A good/service is distinct if: (1) the customer can benefit from it on its own, AND (2) it is separately identifiable from other promises.
Example: A software company sells software + installation + 2-year support for $12,000. These are THREE separate POs (each is distinct).

v: Fixed consideration: straightforward
Variable consideration (refunds, rebates, bonuses): estimate using EXPECTED VALUE or MOST LIKELY AMOUNT — include only if highly probable of not reversing
Significant financing component: adjust for time value if payment > 12 months from delivery
Non-cash consideration: measure at fair value

1-31.v: Revenue allocated to PO = (Standalone price of PO / Total standalone prices of all POs) × Total Transaction Price
Example: Software $8,000 SSP, Installation $2,000 SSP, Support $4,000 SSP. Total SSP = $14,000. Contract price $12,000. Software allocation = (8,000/14,000) × $12,000 = $6,857.

r: Control transfers at a specific moment
Product delivery, title transfer, customer acceptance
Customer simultaneously receives and consumes benefits, company creates asset customer controls, or no alternative use + right to payment
Long-term construction contracts, SaaS subscriptions, maintenance services
Right to consideration EARNED but NOT yet billed
Completed milestone but invoice not yet sent
Cash received BEFORE performance obligation satisfied
Invoice sent — only passage of time needed

v: Portfolio approach: Apply ASC 606 to a portfolio of similar contracts if effects would not differ materially
Significant financing component: Ignore if payment expected within 12 months
Sales taxes collected: Exclude from transaction price (acting as agent for government)
Shipping after control transfers: Accounting policy to treat as fulfilment cost rather than separate PO

1-31.v: Exam Tips: 5 steps — always in order. Distinct = can benefit on own AND separately identifiable. Control transfer = key trigger (not risks & rewards). Variable consideration = constrain to highly probable amount. Over time recognition requires one of 3 criteria. Contract liability = cash received before performance. Contract asset = performance before billing. Relative SSP method for allocation.

1-32.v: Bonds are long-term debt instruments where the issuer (borrower) promises to pay periodic INTEREST (coupon payments) and repay the FACE VALUE (principal) at maturity. Bonds are issued at par, premium, or discount depending on how the coupon rate compares to the market interest rate.

r: Carrying value decreases to face over life
Carrying value increases to face over life

v: Face Value (Par): Amount paid to bondholders at maturity — typically $1,000 per bond
Coupon Rate (Stated Rate): Rate used to calculate the cash interest payment
Market Rate (Effective Rate/Yield): Rate investors require — used for present value calculations
Issue Price: Present value of all future cash flows discounted at the MARKET rate
Carrying Value (Book Value): Face value ± unamortised premium or discount

1-32.v: Cash Interest = Face Value × Coupon Rate × (Time Period)
Interest Expense = Carrying Value (beginning of period) × Market Rate × (Time Period)
The effective interest method is REQUIRED under both GAAP and IFRS. It produces a constant interest RATE each period (not constant interest expense in dollar terms).
This example: face $100,000, coupon 8%, market 10%, issued at discount. Interest expense INCREASES each period as the carrying value increases toward face value.

v: ISSUE at discount: Dr. Cash (proceeds) / Dr. Discount on BP (difference) / Cr. Bonds Payable (face)
ISSUE at premium: Dr. Cash (proceeds) / Cr. Premium on BP (difference) / Cr. Bonds Payable (face)
INTEREST PAYMENT: Dr. Interest Expense (effective) / Cr. Discount on BP (amortisation) / Cr. Cash (coupon) [for discount bond]
MATURITY: Dr. Bonds Payable (face) / Cr. Cash (face)

1-32.v: Gain/Loss = Carrying Value at Retirement Date − Cash Paid to Retire | Gain if CV > Cash Paid | Loss if CV < Cash Paid
Example: Bonds with carrying value $98,000 are retired by paying $101,000 in cash. Loss on retirement = $101,000 - $98,000 = $3,000 loss (included in income from continuing operations).

Exam Tips: Coupon > Market Rate → Premium → CV decreases each period. Coupon < Market Rate → Discount → CV increases each period. Effective interest method = CV × market rate = interest expense. Amortisation = Interest Expense - Cash Paid (discount) OR Cash Paid - Interest Expense (premium). Early retirement: Gain if CV > price paid; Loss if price paid > CV. At maturity, CV ALWAYS equals face value.

1-33.v: EPS is the most widely used measure of a company's profitability on a per-share basis. Public companies must report BOTH basic and diluted EPS on the face of the income statement. EPS drives P/E ratios and investor valuations.
Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding

v: Preferred dividends are subtracted — EPS belongs to COMMON shareholders only
Cumulative preferred: subtract dividends whether declared or not
Non-cumulative preferred: subtract only if declared
Weighted average shares: weight shares by fraction of year outstanding

1-33.v: Weighted Average Shares — How to Calculate
Example: Jan 1 — 100,000 shares. Apr 1 — issued 30,000 new shares. Oct 1 — repurchased 12,000 shares (treasury).
Stock Splits & Stock Dividends — Retroactive Adjustment
Stock splits and stock dividends are treated as if they occurred at the BEGINNING OF THE EARLIEST PERIOD PRESENTED. Retroactively adjust ALL prior period share counts — no weighting needed.

Example: 100,000 shares Jan 1. Issued 30,000 Apr 1. 2-for-1 stock split Dec 1. All shares before split are DOUBLED retroactively: 100,000 × 2 = 200,000 base; 30,000 × 2 = 60,000 issued Apr 1. Weighted average = recalculated with doubled figures.
Diluted EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends + Adjustments) / (Weighted Avg Shares + Dilutive Potential Shares)
Diluted EPS shows what EPS would be if ALL dilutive potential shares were exercised. It is always ≤ Basic EPS (or equal if no dilutive securities exist).
Assumes options/warrants are exercised at the exercise price, and the proceeds are used to repurchase shares at the AVERAGE MARKET PRICE during the period.
Net New Shares = Shares Issued on Exercise − Shares Repurchased with Proceeds | = Options Outstanding − (Options × Exercise Price / Avg Market Price)

Example: 10,000 options, exercise price $20, avg market price $25. Shares issued = 10,000. Proceeds = 10,000 × $20 = $200,000. Shares repurchased = $200,000 / $25 = 8,000. Net new dilutive shares = 10,000 − 8,000 = 2,000.
If including a security INCREASES EPS (makes it HIGHER than Basic EPS), it is ANTI-DILUTIVE and EXCLUDED from diluted EPS.

v: Options/warrants are anti-dilutive when exercise price > average market price (treasury stock method creates negative shares which would increase EPS)
Convertible bonds are anti-dilutive when the EPS impact of conversion (add-back interest / new shares) > Basic EPS
ALWAYS check: only include securities that reduce EPS

1-33.v: Exam Tips: Basic EPS = (NI - Preferred Div) / Weighted Avg Shares. Diluted ≤ Basic always. Stock split/dividend = retroactive adjustment to ALL periods. Treasury stock method for options: proceeds repurchase shares at AVG market price. Anti-dilutive = excluded from diluted EPS. Cumulative preferred = subtract whether declared or not. If exercise price > market price = anti-dilutive options.
